coredata.dc:title: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) modified metal-organic frameworks boosting carbon dots electrochemiluminescence emission for sensitive miRNA detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Honghong
Liu, Pengfei
Peng, Jiaxin
Yu, Haoming
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Highly efficient luminescent materials play an important role in electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensing systems. Herein, the poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) modified carbon dots (CDs)/zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) compositing metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) materials with excellent luminescence performance were prepared as the ECL emitters for biosensing application. In this novel ternary composites, CDs were used as emitters, ZIF-8 was used as a carrier, and the luminescent performance was finally improved by introducing PEDOT:PSS to improve the conductivity of the nanomaterials. As a result, CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 exhibited an approximately 8 times ECL intensity compared to CDs alone. By further modifying with AuNPs, the enhancement factor reached ≈10 in reference to the individual CDs. After combining with a DNAzyme-based two-cycle target amplification principle, an ECL biosensor was constructed to achieve high-sensitivity detection of miRNA-21 with a detection limit of 50 aM. The biosensor also demonstrated desirable selectivity, excellent stability, and quantitative ability for human serum target detection. Overall, these findings not only provide a promising pathway for high luminous efficiency ECL emitters synthesis, but also provide a platform for ultrasensitive miRNA sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
PEDOT:PSS
ZIF-8
Electrochemiluminescence
miRNA-21

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and repeatability of the proposed biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CDs, (B) ZIF-8, (C)PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8, (D) CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8. (E) SEM image of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs. (F) Corresponding elemental mapping images of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs. (G) XRD pattern of nanomaterials. (H) FT-IR spectrum of nanomaterials. (I) Fluorescence spectrum of nanomaterials (excitation at 420 nm). (J) UV–vis absorption spectra of nanomaterials.
(A) The possible ECL mechanism of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/GE. (B) ECL responses and (C) CV curves of nanomaterials in 0.1 M PBS (inclusive 0.1 M K₂S₂O₈). All ECL and CV scanning voltage was −1.6–0 V.

Evaluation of electrical conductivity of nanomaterials: (A) CV and (B) EIS behaviors of nanomaterials. TEM images of the nanomaterials after immersion in pure water solution for 48h at room temperature: (C) ZIF-8, (D) PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8, and (E) CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8. (F) ECL signal stability of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 composites.

Native PAGE images of different samples. (A) PAGE validation of the cyclic toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification process. (B) PAGE characterization of Mn2+-driven DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage.

(A) ECL intensity-time curves of different concentrations of miRNA-21 (0, 100 aM, 1 fM, 10 fM, 100 fM, 1 pM,10 pM,100 pM). (B) The linear relationship between ΔI and the logarithm of the target concentration. (C) Evaluation of the specificity of the ECL biosensor detection strategy. (D) Repeatability of the ECL biosensor. Target miRNA-21: 100 fM. Error bar means SD (n = 3).
Working principle of the biosensor. (A). The preparation process of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs. (B) Schematic of the Mn2+-driven DNAzyme-catalyzed ECL biosensor for miRNA-21 detection.

head.ce:title: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) modified metal-organic frameworks boosting carbon dots electrochemiluminescence emission for sensitive miRNA detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly efficient luminescent materials play an important role in electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensing systems. Herein, the poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) modified carbon dots (CDs)/zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) compositing metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) materials with excellent luminescence performance were prepared as the ECL emitters for biosensing application. In this novel ternary composites, CDs were used as emitters, ZIF-8 was used as a carrier, and the luminescent performance was finally improved by introducing PEDOT:PSS to improve the conductivity of the nanomaterials. As a result, CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 exhibited an approximately 8 times ECL intensity compared to CDs alone. By further modifying with AuNPs, the enhancement factor reached ≈10 in reference to the individual CDs. After combining with a DNAzyme-based two-cycle target amplification principle, an ECL biosensor was constructed to achieve high-sensitivity detection of miRNA-21 with a detection limit of 50 aM. The biosensor also demonstrated desirable selectivity, excellent stability, and quantitative ability for human serum target detection. Overall, these findings not only provide a promising pathway for high luminous efficiency ECL emitters synthesis, but also provide a platform for ultrasensitive miRNA sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a promising analysis tool that marriage the advantages of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence, with the virtues of near-zero background noise, superb sensitivity, wide dynamic ranges, and ease of integration, has been widely used in various fields such as environmental analysis, medical diagnostics, and food security (Bertolino et al., 2005; C. Li et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2011; Yoo et al., 2022). In the ECL reaction system, ECL emitters are the main components and play a key role in the analytical performance of the developed biosensor (Li et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2023). Considering this attribute, substantial efforts have been devoted to exploring highly efficient ECL emitters in the field of ECL for building sensitive sensors and analysis over the past decades.

With the development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, nanomaterials with outstanding electrical, optical, and catalytic properties and large specific surface areas have provided opportunities for manufacturing highly efficient ECL emitters (Bertoncello and Forster, 2009; Chen et al., 2017). Various attractive nanomaterials such as QDs, metal nanoclusters, metal halide perovskites, and carbon-based nanomaterials have been developed as novel emitters for the construction of ultrasensitive ECL biosensors and bioanalysis (Feng et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2018; Zou et al., 2020). Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging type of carbon nanomaterials with exquisite size below 10 nm, along with the virtues of eminent luminous efficiency, wonderful stability, low toxicity, and facile fabrication, hold a greatly promising application in biosensing and bioimaging (Caglayan et al., 2022; Ji et al., 2020). Unfortunately, CDs showed weak ECL emissions, which significantly reduces their practical value in the ECL field (Dong et al., 2010; Yang et al., 2013).

Seeking suitable support to carry many luminophores is one of the most efficient ways to enhance the emission of luminescent materials (C.-C. Li et al., 2022). In this regard, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a novel class of porous nanomaterials, with unprecedented chemical and structural tunability, are considered promising candidates (Falcaro et al., 2011; Jiang et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020). Indeed, scientists have developed several strategies to synthesize MOF-based nanocomposites by doping, postmodification, or encapsulation of luminescent molecules including Ru(bpy)3 2+, luminol, and nanoluminophores for sensitive ECL biosensing applications (Hu et al., 2018; Liao et al., 2019; Wang, 2017; Xiong et al., 2019). Among them, zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8), a classical MOF composed of the coordination bonds of zinc ions and 2-methylimidazole ligands, has gained remarkable attention because of its porosity, high stability, good biocompatibility, large surface area, and catalytic activity (Ding et al., 2021; Fang et al., 2020; Park et al., 2006; Sun et al., 2022; Trino et al., 2021). Our group has showcased the feasibility of using ZIF-8 to enhance the CDs ECL (Liang et al., 2021). Nevertheless, ZIFs have inherent poor conductivity, which has yielded only marginal improvements in ECL luminescent performance (Liang et al., 2021; Tao et al., 2022). Very recently, scientists have demonstrated the possibility of using an excellent ion-electron conductor, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) matrix to improve the conductivity of nanomaterials (Cheng et al., 2016; Schultheiss et al., 2020). This inspires the scientists to utilize PEDOT:PSS modified MOFs for signal translating devices in various bioelectronic devices, yet, their ECL biosensing and bioanalysis application remains scarce.

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are short (18–22 nucleotides) non-coding RNAs that play important roles in the regulation of gene expression at the post-transcriptional level (Catalanotto et al., 2016; Duan et al., 2020; Goryacheva et al., 2019; Qian et al., 2017). It was reported that miRNAs regulate more than 30% of the human genome and are involved in almost all fundamental cell processes (Chen et al., 2008). They usually exhibit aberrant expression under pathological conditions and have been considered potential biomarkers for many diseases (Aw et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2023; Jet et al., 2021). Research in infectious diseases has also revealed that miRNAs can be biomarkers for viral or non-viral infections (Jiao et al., 2017). Consequently, miRNA detection has become an emerging field in diagnostics and prognostics monitoring (Song et al., 2021). However, conventional technologies face challenges in detecting miRNAs due to their inherent features (Y. Li et al., 2022). First, the low abundance of miRNAs needs labor/resource-intensive sample enrichment, which hinders individual investigations. Second, the small size of miRNAs makes amplification techniques (e.g., reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR)) prone to give false positive results owing to amplification bias (Li et al., 2014). In addition, high similarity in sequences among miRNA family members (some differ by only a single base) needs high specificity detection, traditional techniques are not always sufficient to distinguish them (Deng et al., 2017). Therefore, there is a pressing need for the development of an ultrasensitive, specific, and robust approach for miRNA detection.

Herein, PEDOT:PSS modified CDs/ZIF-8 was synthesized as highly-efficiency ECL emitters, and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were further modified for biolabeling (Scheme 1 A), followed by a combination with target amplification to construct an ECL biosensor for detection of miRNA-21 (Scheme 1B). Experimental data demonstrated that CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs produced significantly increased ECL emission compared with individual CDs, and the biosensor exhibited super sensitivity with a low limit of detection of 50 aM, which provides a promising pathway for high luminous efficiency ECL emitters synthesis and trace biomarkers detection.
CDs were synthesized by a microwave-assistant method, detailed procedures can be found in our previous report (Liu et al., 2020).

The synthesis of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 was accorded to the previous report with a major modification (Guo et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019). At first, the ultrapure water was mixed with ethanol with a volume ratio of 3:2 and served as the solvent of zinc nitrate and 2-aminoethanol. Then, an equal volume of zinc nitrate solution (4 mM) and 2-aminoethanol solution (1.6 mM) was quickly mixed and aged at ambient temperature for at least 30 min to obtain positively charged zinc hydroxide nanostrands (ZHNs). Subsequently, 4 mL negatively charged PEDOT:PSS diluted 500 times with ultrapure water was injected into as-synthesized positively charged ZHNs solution (5 mL) and the mixture was stirred for 10 min to complete electrostatic interaction. Then, the solution containing PEDOT:PSS/ZHNs composites formed. Afterward, 1 mL as-prepared CDs solution, and 2 mL 2-MI solution (24 mM) which was prepared by dissolving in miscible solvents of ultrapure water and ethanol with a volume ratio of 4:1, were simultaneously introduced into the PEDOT:PSS/ZHNs composites solution and allowed to incubate at ambient temperature for 24 h. After the reaction, the earthy yellow precipitates were treated with a centrifugation and wash procedure with the mixture of ultrapure water and ethanol with a volume ratio of 3:2 three times. Then, the obtained CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 composites were dried at 60 °C and stored in a dark environment.

AuNPs were synthesized on the surface of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 by in situ reduction method. Generally, 20 mg as-prepared CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 was dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water. Then, 12.5 μL HAuCl₄ (1%) was injected into the above solution and mixed thoroughly. With stirring continuously, 25 μL NaBH₄ (0.01 M) was added to the mixture with continuous stirring and reacted for 20 min under the protection of a series of nitrogen. Then, 10 μL trisodium citrate (0.01 M) was introduced into the solution and allowed to react for another 30 min. Finally, the reaction product was centrifuged and washed, and redispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water for later use.

The fabrication procedure of the biosensor was demonstrated in Scheme 1B. All sequences of oligonucleotides were showed in Table S1. Before use, the GE was initially polished with aluminum oxide powder to form a slip surface, followed by sonicating with ethanol and ultrapure water successively. After drying thoroughly under nitrogen, the electrode was further cleaned with an electrochemical method. Then, 10 μL prepared CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs composites were directly dropped on the interface of the GE and dried at ambient temperature. Afterward, 4 μM thiolated Fc-tagged track DNA hairpin probe and 400 nM locking/DNAzyme duplexes, which were pretreated with TCEP (10 mM) to reduce disulfide bond, were simultaneously modified on the CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs surface via Au-thiol bond. As a slightly wet state was seen on the as-modified GE, a total volume of 10 μL MCH (1 mM) was dripped onto the electrode to seal the bare sites and remove loosely tethered DNA. Through a washing process with PBS (pH 7.4), the electrode was consummately fabricated and preserved at 4 °C for further experiment.

The entire target detection process was based on a cascade signal amplification, which can be divided into two parts (Scheme 1B). For the first portion, 10 μL various concentrations of miRNA-21 were appended in the equal volume of the as-prepared substrate/output/waste triplex complexes (1 nM) and incubated with 1 nM fuel strand at 37 °C for 40 min to power the circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification (final volume of 30 μL). For the second portion, half the volume of all the above resultant solutions was dripped on the well-modified electrodes. Meanwhile, 5 μL Mn2+ (10 mM) was cast onto the electrode surface and incubated at room temperature for 60 min to ensure the DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage. After the reaction, the electrode was washed with PBS and finally transferred into an ECL testing solution for measurement.

To obtain the high luminescent ECL emitters, a series of nanomaterials including CDs, ZIF-8, CDs/ZIF-8, CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8, and CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs, were prepared. The structures and morphologies of nanomaterials were first assessed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs were uniformly distributed spheroid-like nanoparticles with diameters less than 5 nm, moreover, CDs have excellent optical stability in different buffers (Fig. S1). And in Fig. 1B, the single ZIF-8 exhibited a regular dodecahedron shape with about 200 nm in diameter. Derived from that, the synthesized PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 and CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 still retained the dodecahedron shape but the particles became adhesive in the presence of the PEDOT:PSS matrix (Fig. 1C and D). For CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs composites, it was worth noting that the tiny AuNPs were observed on the surface ZIF-8 dodecahedron (Fig. 1E). which was further confirmed by a typical AuNPs UV–vis absorption feature at 520 nm (Fig. 1J). Elemental mapping images also demonstrated that Au, S, Zn, C, O, and N were evenly distributed over the entire CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs (Fig. 1F, Fig. S2). The crystal structures of CDs, CDs/ZIF-8, CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8, and CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs were further characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. All the as-synthesized ZIF-8-composites displayed the typical XRD patterns of standard ZIF-8, and there was no change at the position of the diffraction peak, showing that the crystallinity of ZIF-8 didn't destroy after doping the CDs and PEDOT:PSS, which further demonstrated the successful combination of CDs, PEDOT:PSS, ZIF-8 together (Fig. 1G). The functional groups of the materials were further analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), as shown in Fig. 1H, the characteristic peak at around 3000 and 1700 cm−1, regarded as the typical –OH, N–H, CO bonds, was observed in CDs. ZIF-8 displayed characteristic absorption peaks at 1580 cm−1, 1698 cm−1, 2927 cm−1, 3135 cm−1, and 3415 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CC, CO, NH₂, and N–H bonds respectively, 1458 and 1420 cm−1 peaks corresponded to the bending vibration of the N–H bond. Notably, the non-observed absorption peaks of CDs in CDs/ZIF-8 complexes may be because of that the CDs were encapsulated within ZIF-8 rather than being physically adsorbed onto its surface or the limited loading CDs were negligible to provide observable characteristic absorption peaks. Next, we sought to verify whether CDs were encapsulated within the ZIF-8 scaffold and to measure the CDs' loading amount. The CDs loading efficiency was surveyed through the measurement of the concentration differences of the supernatants before and after assembly using UV–vis assay (Fig. S3A), and the results showed a ca. 16% wt CDs loading. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) N2 adsorption–desorption technique was applied to get an insight into the pore-level of ZIF-8 and CDs/ZIF-8 (Fig. S3B). The determined (BET) surface area value of CDs/ZIF-8 (1092.4 m2 g−1) was quite similar to the pure ZIF-8 (1216.6 m2 g−1), evidencing that the CDs were embedded into ZIF-8 during the preparation. Finally, the encapsulation of CDs within ZIF-8 enabled all CDs/ZIF-8 composites to have similar UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra with the single CDs, indicating that the structures of luminescence CDs did not change when they were compositing with ZIF-8 (Fig. 1I and J).

As well known, the cathodic ECL of CDs is assisted by the reduction of S2O8 2−, therefore, the performance of all prepared CDs-based nanomaterials in the K₂S₂O₈ ECL reaction system was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 B, all three prepared CDs doped ZIF-8 composites achieved strong ECL emissions, while the individual CDs obtained a relatively weak ECL signal. It is noteworthy that the PEDOT:PSS modified nanomaterials showed greatly improved ECL, the enhancement factor was ≈8 (enhancement factor: the multiple between the ECL intensity of the composite and that of the CDs alone), while non-PEDOT:PSS CDs/ZIF-8 was only ≈ 2. This significant enhancement may benefit from the high conductivity PEDOT:PSS accelerates the ECL reaction (Fig. 2A). Briefly, during the cathodic ECL process, both emitters CDs and co-reactant S2O8 2− were electro-reduced to radical intermediate CDs•– and SO4 •–. The PEDOT:PSS has high conductivity, which could promote the electron transfer between the electrode and reagents, and accelerate these electro-reduced reactions. Such a hypothesis was further supported by the corresponding positively shifted potential of S2O8 2− (from −0.9 V to −0.6 V) and the stronger reduction current of CDs (at −1.6 V) (Fig. 2C). After further modification with AuNPs, the nanocomposites revealed further increased emission, and the enhancement factor reached ≈10, owing to the high conductivity of AuNPs (Fig. 2B).

The possible ECL mechanism of PEDOT:PSS accelerates the reaction was further investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). CV is the effective method to study the electron transfer kinetics of the system (Dong et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2023). Four electrode systems were constructed, wherein the redox peaks of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 and CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs as the working electrode were higher than that of the ZIF-8 and CDs/ZIF-8 electrodes in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (Fig. 3 A). This can be attributed to the conductive polymer PEDOT:PSS, which increased the electron transfer rate and endowed nanomaterials with higher electrical activity, making it suitable for electrochemical sensing applications. EIS is another method that can provide useful information for electron transfer reactions (Lin et al., 2023). Fig. 3B presented the impedance values for the ZIF-8 electrode, ZIF-8/CDs electrode, CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 electrode, and CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs electrode. Compared to pure ZIF-8 electrode and ZIF-8/CDs electrode, the PEDOT:PSS-containing materials electrodes had lower electrochemical impedance at high frequencies, suggesting that charge transfer through PEDOT:PSS-containing materials is easier. These results revealed that the conductive polymer PEDOT:PSS could facilitate the enhancement of CDs ECL reaction.

Overall, the combination of highly conductive materials with the porous structure of MOFs could significantly improve the ECL luminescence intensity of CDs. To further evaluate the stability of ZIF-8, PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8, and CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 materials, the morphology of the materials immersed in water for 48 h was observed. It could be observed that compared with the TEM image in Fig. 1, the shapes of ZIF-8 and PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 changed slightly, while the morphology of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 did not change and remained dodecahedral, indicating that the addition of CDs improved the stability of ZIF-8 (Fig. 3C, D, and E). Subsequently, the ECL signal stability of the prepared CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 was tested for 15 days. Fig. 3F showed no significant fluctuation in the ECL signal, indicating that the CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 material has excellent stability.

The principle of the proposed ECL biosensor demonstrated in Scheme 1B can be systematically divided into two portions. One is circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification, and the other is Mn2+-driven DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage. For the first portion, the substrate strand, waste strand, and output strand were self-assembled to a triplex complex, in which the termination of the substrate strand exposed a toehold domain 1 for target miRNA-21 hybridization. The presented miRNA-21 hybridized with the substrate strand through the toehold and released the waste strand. At the same time, a new toehold domain 2 for fuel strand hybridization was generated. The fuel strand could recognize the new toehold domain hybridize with that, and trigger branch migration to form a substrate/fuel duplex. As a consequence, target miRNA-21 and output strand were simultaneously released from the substrate strand. Then, target miRNA-21 participated in a new circle and released another waste strand. Upon the addition of the fuel strand, the output strand was also released. N circles later, plentiful output strands were produced. Given the unique properties of the circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification, one target strand could generate a large number of dissociative output strands, achieving the first-stage amplification.

For the second-stage amplification, Mn2+-driven DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage was introduced. As shown in Scheme 1B, the synthesized CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs composites were dripped onto the GE to obtain an original ECL signal. Then, thiolated ferrocene (Fc)-tagged track hairpin strand and locking/DNAzyme duplexes with a certain ratio of 10:1 were synchronously modified on the surface of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs composites via Au-thiol chemistry bond, in which the Fc-tagged on the termination of the hairpin strand was used to quench the emission of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs composites. Afterward, a large number of dissociative output strands produced by circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification were introduced on the electrode to hybridize with the locking strand, leading to the dissociation of the locking strand from the DNAzyme strand. Consequently, the activated DNAzyme strand would bind to track hairpin strands and subsequently cleave the hairpin strand into two fragments with the aid of Mn2+. After dissociating from the fragments, the DNAzyme strand bound to another hairpin strand and cleaved it until no hairpin strand was accessible. During the Mn2+-driven DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage process, Fc-tagged fragments were kept away from the surface of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs composites, and the emission of those was gradually recovered. Benefiting from the cascade signal amplification, hence, the quantitative detection could be achieved since the concentration of target miRNA-21 was directly proportional to the recovered ECL signal.

The native PAGE was employed to assess the reaction feasibility of the cascade signal amplification from two portions. For the circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification, the result was depicted in Fig. 4 A. Lanes 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 corresponded to the target miRNA-21, output strand, substrate strand, waste strand, and fuel strand, respectively, which were used as the controls. Lanes 6 and 7 were substrate/output binary hybrid chains and substrate/output/waste ternary hybrid chains respectively. The addition of the target miRNA-21 into the solution containing the substrate/output/waste triplex produced a new intermediate substrate/output/miRNA-21 band and a free waste chain (lane 8), in which the band of substrate/output/miRNA-21 triplex was consistent with natural that in lane 9. This indicated that miRNA-21 can displace waste from the substrate/output/waste ternary hybrid chain. Moreover, incubating the substrate/output/miRNA-21 triplex with the fuel strand, the free miRNA-21, output strand and the substrate/fuel binary hybrid chain could be observed (lane 10), in which the binary hybrid chain was identical to the natural substrate/fuel hybrid chain in lane 11. Therefore, the circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification is feasible as the target miRNA-21 amplifier.

Additionally, the second-stage amplification was also evaluated by native PAGE (Fig. 4B). The output strand, locking strand, DNAzyme strand, and track hairpin strand were utilized as the controls (lanes 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively). After incubating the locking strand with the DNAzyme strand, a new band was generated, which corresponded to the locking/DNAzyme hybrids (lane 5). With the addition of the output strand, the bands of locking/DNAzyme disappeared (lane 6) and a new band corresponding to the output/locking duplex formed (lane 7). Upon DNAzyme was incubated with hairpin strand as well as cofactor Mn2+, two new bands were obtained, in which the bands were cleavage fragments generated by Mn2+-driven DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage (lane 8). Noting that one single-stranded fragment was incapable of being observed owing to a low weight. These above results demonstrated that target miRNA-21 released waste strand, fuel strand released target miRNA-21 and output strand, output strand hybridized with locking strand and activated DNAzyme strand, DNAzyme strand cleaved track hairpin strand, which thus could be used as cascade signal amplification for target miRNA-21 detection with high sensitivity. Electrochemical characterization of the proposed biosensor was also performed by ECL and EIS, the results were presented in Fig. S4.

Upon the best conditions (Fig. S5), the detection performance of the cascade signal amplification strategy-based ECL biosensor was investigated. Initially, various concentrations of miRNA-21 were introduced into substrate/output/waste triplex with the external addition of fuel strand and incubated at ambient temperature for 40 min. Half of the resultant solutions were subsequently dripped on the electrode surface and incubated for 60 min. After washing with PBS, the electrode was measured in an ECL testing solution. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the recovered ECL intensity experienced an upward trend when target miRNA-21 concentrated from blank to 100 pM. Additionally, a linear relationship between the change value of ECL intensity (ΔI) and the logarithm of the concentration of target miRNA-21 (lgC) was revealed in Fig. 5B. The linear regression equation was ΔI = 2014.2 lgC +33899.6 (R2 = 0.9985), while the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as low as 50 aM (detailed information was shown in supporting information), which is better than most of the reported biosensors (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and repeatability of the proposed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, we researched the specificity and repeatability of the proposed biosensor. A series of interfering agents were recruited to measure their corresponding ECL recoveries under the identical experimental environment including blank control, miRNA-155, miRNA-141, miRNA-17, and their mixture. As depicted in Fig. 5C, the individual interfering agent exhibited an almost negligible change in ECL intensity similar to the blank control, indicating that no cascade signal amplification occurred. In contrast, the target miRNA-21 individually and the mixture containing target miRNA-21 revealed a dramatic change of ECL signal, which indicated that only miRNA-21 could trigger circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification and subsequent DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage. In addition, to investigate the repeatability of the proposed biosensor, five parallel experiments were arranged under an identical environment. Results in Fig. 5D showed that a trivial difference in the change of ECL value could be seen among these parallel experiments, demonstrating a desirable repeatability of the proposed biosensor.

To assess the practical clinical application of high-conductivity CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs composites-based and the cascade signal amplification-assisted detection strategy, human serum was recruited as the real sample. Then, a series of concentrations of target miRNA-21 were spiked in the human serum samples, experienced circular toehold-mediated strand displacement amplification and Mn2+-driven DNAzyme-catalyzed cleavage, and measured in the ECL testing solution. As exhibited in Table S3, the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 2.3 to 5.1%, while the recoveries were in the range of 94.3–102.1%. The results suggested the excellent practicability of the detection strategy in real samples and held great promise in clinical applications.

In summary, the novel high-efficiency ECL emitters CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8 were prepared and applied to construct an ultrasensitive biosensor. For the novel emitters, the topological order and porous structure of ZIF-8 enable the locating and uniform distribution of CDs in the framework; the high conductivity PEDOT:PSS and AuNPs promoted electron transfer between the electrode and nanomaterials, promoting the electrochemical activation of CDs that adsorbed in the ZIF-8 framework. As a result, the as-prepared CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs exhibited a 10-fold higher ECL emission compared to the individual CDs. Based on the excellent ECL performance and easy labeling property of CDs/PEDOT:PSS/ZIF-8/AuNPs, a novel ECL biosensor was successfully constructed by combining the novel composites with DNAzyme-based two-cycle target amplification, achieving ultrasensitive and high-selectivity determination of miRNA-21. In short, this work provided an effective method to design and prepare high-performance ECL materials, and expand the application of PEDOT:PSS and MOFs in the field of biosensing.

Honghong Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Pengfei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Jiaxin Peng: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Haoming Yu: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Li Wang: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
